summary: This post compiles reader feedback on Joel Spolsky's original programmer compensation article, covering debates about level systems, startup bonuses, stock options, equal pay within levels, and individual incentives. Readers challenge specific aspects like the level 13-14 criteria, the effectiveness of startup bonuses as salary gap bridges, and whether equal pay within levels kills motivation for top performers. Naval Ravikant suggests profit sharing over stock options to avoid the common problem of mediocre early employees sitting on outsized equity. Several readers advocate for flexible benefits, training budgets, and transparency as key compensation factors. Joel notes he revised his compensation policy based on this feedback and reaffirms his opposition to individual performance bonuses.
key_points: Naval Ravikant argues profit sharing is superior to stock options because allocation mistakes are correctable yearly rather than permanent, avoiding the common problem of early mediocre employees hoarding outsized equity
Multiple readers challenge equal pay within levels, arguing it removes individual motivation and frustrates top performers, while Joel counters that he'd simply bump exceptional performers up a level

Dave Polaschek advocates for cafeteria-style benefits where employees allocate a percentage of salary across health insurance, training, vacation, and other options rather than one-size-fits-all packages

Several readers emphasize that salary transparency and open compensation policies reduce workplace tension more than secrecy, with one noting his company's stock option amounts were kept secret despite being simply based on salary grade
The startup bonus concept draws criticism for attracting mercenary hires and creating a compensation structure where longer-tenured employees fall behind market rates
money_quotes: Companies that are slow to hire and quick to fire stand a better chance of insuring they have credible, reliable technology staffs.
The situation that you're trying to avoid is having someone mediocre but early sitting on a ton of options whereas someone who has joined much later but is doing a great job has far less stock.
You can share a very large percentage of the company's profits when the company is young, and slowly bring the percentage down over time as the company matures.
If a salary of X doesn't make me happy now, as an 'unknown risk' to your team, I'm going to be less happy with it down the road once my value to the team has been well-established.
One way of looking at salaries is asking yourself: 'What would I have to pay to contract this programmer back six months from now after she leaves because we don't pay her enough now?'
Secrets do more harm than good. Keeping it open and telling everybody up front that it's open will keep it from interfering with the work.
He gave me a massive bonus and said that he likes to give bonuses BEFORE the success – he finds it's much more concrete motivation. Now this is a bit of a dirty trick but it sure does work.
Being fair is a good ideal, but because of the special cases, equal pay is unlikely to be fair.
key_insight: Fair programmer compensation requires transparency and systematic structure, but rigid equality within levels inevitably collides with the reality that individual contributions, circumstances, and market conditions vary enormously.
